jokes.text: When you have meetings with her lawyer, always wear the same suit. That way they think it's the only one you own. They don't rape you so much on the fucking alimony.
jokes.setup: Artie gives divorce advice to Hank
jokes.punchline: The cynical strategy of appearing poor to reduce alimony payments
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - cynical, practical, and darkly funny. The specificity of the advice and crude language make it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: When you have meetings with her lawyer, always wear the same suit. That way they think it's the only one you own. They don't rape you so much on the fucking alimony.

jokes.text: That is what Chinese man say when he go to dentist. He say, 'uh-oh. Uh-oh, my tooth hurting.'
jokes.setup: Paula mentions 2:30 appointment time
jokes.punchline: Hank makes a 'tooth-hurty' pun with racist accent
jokes.explanation: Hacky racist joke that feels dated and lazy. Shows Hank's tendency toward inappropriate humor but poorly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That is what Chinese man say when he go to dentist. He say, 'uh-oh. Uh-oh, my tooth hurting.'

jokes.text: Let us blow Mr. Neil Callaham's mind.
jokes.setup: Hank preparing to call a hospitalized fan
jokes.punchline: The overblown language for a simple courtesy call
jokes.explanation: Hank's self-importance shining through - treating a fan call like a major event

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Let us blow Mr. Neil Callaham's mind.

jokes.text: Callaham.
jokes.setup: Hank calls to cheer up a hospitalized fan
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable pause suggests the fan has died
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - the single word 'Callaham' and pause tells the whole story. Perfect Larry Sanders Show awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Callaham.

jokes.text: No, the first one kind of took the wind out of my sails.
jokes.setup: After learning the fan died
jokes.punchline: Hank's massive understatement about the devastating news
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank Kingsley - his self-absorbed response to tragedy is both horrible and hilarious. Great character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, the first one kind of took the wind out of my sails.

jokes.text: Do I call you Sarah or Sarah Jessica Parker? Or Sarah Jessica? Whatever you'd like. Really. Well, I'll call you Sal.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about proper name usage
jokes.punchline: He settles on the completely unrelated nickname 'Sal'
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry awkwardness - overthinking a simple question then arriving at an illogical conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Do I call you Sarah or Sarah Jessica Parker? Or Sarah Jessica?

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Jessica Parker
dialogue.line: Whatever you'd like. Really.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, I'll call you Sal.

jokes.text: You know, we had Patricia Arquette on the show the other night. And she died the death of a dog.
jokes.setup: Hank compliments Sarah Jessica Parker
jokes.punchline: He immediately trashes another actress unprompted
jokes.explanation: Shows Hank's complete lack of social awareness - complimenting someone by insulting someone else. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, we had Patricia Arquette on the show the other night. And she died the death of a dog.

jokes.text: I have a boyfriend.
jokes.setup: Hank's inappropriate rambling about young actresses
jokes.punchline: SJP's immediate shutdown of his advances
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - she knows where this is heading and cuts it off. The economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Jessica Parker
dialogue.line: I have a boyfriend.

jokes.text: I thought we could shoot a few games. You know, I got my own cue and balls.
jokes.setup: Hank invites her to play pool
jokes.punchline: The unintentionally sexual phrasing
jokes.explanation: Obvious double entendre but works for Hank's character - he's oblivious to how he sounds.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I thought we could shoot a few games. You know, I got my own cue and balls.

jokes.text: Well, I have my own cue also. He's in New York, and he'll be flying in tonight.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions his pool cue
jokes.punchline: She plays with the double meaning, referring to her boyfriend
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that shows she can match wits with these Hollywood idiots. Well-constructed comeback.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Jessica Parker
dialogue.line: Well, I have my own cue also. He's in New York, and he'll be flying in tonight.

jokes.text: Besides, Hank, you're old enough to be my father. Ouch. How old is your father?
jokes.setup: She says he's old enough to be her father
jokes.punchline: Hank asks how old her father is, missing the insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - completely missing the point of an insult. His 'Ouch' shows he knows it's bad but doesn't understand why.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Jessica Parker
dialogue.line: Besides, Hank, you're old enough to be my father.
Ouch.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: How old is your father?

jokes.text: It's Matthew Broderick. I date Matthew Broderick.
jokes.setup: Continued awkward conversation with Hank
jokes.punchline: Name-dropping her famous boyfriend to shut him down
jokes.explanation: Realistic Hollywood moment - using celebrity status as a shield. Moderately funny but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Sarah Jessica Parker
dialogue.line: It's Matthew Broderick. I date Matthew Broderick.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, no pressure. If tomorrow's better, hey, I'm fly with that. You're fly.
jokes.setup: Hank tries to use current slang
jokes.punchline: SJP mocks his awkward attempt to sound cool
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - middle-aged Hank trying to sound hip and failing miserably.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, no pressure. If tomorrow's better, hey, I'm fly with that. You're fly.

jokes.text: Oh, you hate being hit on by desperate men, too?
jokes.setup: Larry suggests they have something in common
jokes.punchline: She sarcastically identifies their commonality
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that cuts right through Larry's BS. Good economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, you hate being hit on by desperate men, too?

jokes.text: I got a bottle of champagne at home with your name on it. And what name would that be? Brut.
jokes.setup: Hank's cheesy pickup line about champagne
jokes.punchline: She turns it around - the champagne brand becomes the 'name'
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that completely deflates Hank's pickup line. Quick wit and perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I got a bottle of champagne at home with your name on it.
And what name would that be?
Brut.

jokes.text: Oh, I like them small and compact. Then why don't you go fuck a Toyota?
jokes.setup: Hank's creepy comment about her body type
jokes.punchline: She suggests he have sex with a car instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comeback - takes his objectification to its logical extreme. Unexpectedly harsh and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, I like them small and compact.

dialogue.speaker: Stage Manager
dialogue.line: Then why don't you go fuck a Toyota?

jokes.text: Wow, and a sense of humor.
jokes.setup: After being brutally shut down
jokes.punchline: Hank completely misreads the insult as flirtation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - so delusional he thinks being told to fuck a car is charming. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Wow, and a sense of humor.

jokes.text: Wow, and a sense of humor. No one loves me. No one really cares.
jokes.setup: After being brutally rejected
jokes.punchline: Hank's pathetic attempt to spin it as appreciation for humor
jokes.explanation: Pathetic Hank moment - trying to save face after devastating rejection

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Wow, and a sense of humor. No one loves me. No one really cares.

jokes.text: Tight little body. Your breasts. Hank. Hank! Come on.
jokes.setup: Hank complimenting his assistant's hair
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly inappropriate comments
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation structure showing Hank spiraling into harassment

dialogue.line: Tight little body. Your breasts.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Hank! Come on.

jokes.text: After my first wife gave me the gate, I went on a binge of sex, drugs, and 180-proof Everclear that lasted for three years.
jokes.setup: Artie explains divorce recovery process
jokes.punchline: The extreme specificity and duration of his breakdown
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - dark personal history delivered matter-of-factly. The specific alcohol choice adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: After my first wife gave me the gate, I went on a binge of sex, drugs, and 180-proof Everclear that lasted for three years.

jokes.text: After my fourth divorce, I was able to squeeze the same amount of debauchery into a long weekend.
jokes.setup: Following up on his first divorce story
jokes.punchline: He's become more efficient at post-divorce breakdown
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the idea of becoming 'efficient' at self-destruction is perfectly absurd and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: After my fourth divorce, I was able to squeeze the same amount of debauchery into a long weekend.

jokes.text: Shock, denial, fear, booze, boners, acceptance.
jokes.setup: Paula mentions six stages of healing after divorce
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude version of the stages of grief
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of the five stages of grief. 'Boners' in the middle of legitimate emotions is comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Shock, denial, fear, booze, boners, acceptance.

jokes.text: Well, how much just to jack me off? 150 cash up front.
jokes.setup: Hank negotiating with a prostitute
jokes.punchline: The clinical business transaction
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable but necessary for the story. Shows how far Hank has fallen. More dramatic than comedic.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, how much just to jack me off?

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: 150 cash up front.

jokes.text: You know, when you go to a gas station, they ask you to pay first, so it's a precedent, you know.
jokes.setup: Explaining the cash up front policy
jokes.punchline: Comparing prostitution to buying gas
jokes.explanation: Unexpected comparison that normalizes the transaction. Good observational humor from an unlikely source.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: You know, when you go to a gas station, they ask you to pay first, so it's a precedent, you know.

jokes.text: When you go to a gas station, they ask you to pay first, so it's a precedent, you know.
jokes.setup: Explaining the cash-first policy
jokes.punchline: Comparing prostitution to gas station policy
jokes.explanation: Funny comparison that makes the transaction seem almost mundane

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: When you go to a gas station, they ask you to pay first, so it's a precedent, you know.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, hey. No. No kissing. I don't work that way.
jokes.setup: Hank tries to kiss her
jokes.punchline: She has professional boundaries
jokes.explanation: Standard 'Pretty Woman' territory but serves the character arc. More pathetic than funny.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey. No. No kissing. I don't work that way.

jokes.text: Used to make cat noises when I blew him. Jeez. You kind of look like him.
jokes.setup: She mentions a client from Hermosa Beach
jokes.punchline: The bizarre sexual detail and comparison to Hank
jokes.explanation: Weird and uncomfortable detail that adds to the seediness. The comparison to Hank is the real punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: Used to make cat noises when I blew him. Jeez. You kind of look like him.

jokes.text: Rrrr. Mmm. Woof! I'm all right. Aiiee. Arf, arf.
jokes.setup: Hank with the prostitute
jokes.punchline: He makes animal noises during the encounter
jokes.explanation: Callback to the earlier story - Hank becomes the weird client. More disturbing than funny but completes the arc.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Rrrr. Mmm. Woof! I'm all right. Aiiee. Arf, arf.

jokes.text: Hank's not doing the show? How will we survive? Will there be a tomorrow? God in heaven, save us!
jokes.setup: Learning Hank won't be on the show
jokes.punchline: Larry's over-the-top mock concern
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry - his relief disguised as sarcastic concern. Shows the show-business dynamics perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hank's not doing the show? How will we survive? Will there be a tomorrow? God in heaven, save us!

jokes.text: I'm in show business, so I'm a little bit of a whore, too.
jokes.setup: Trying to find common ground with the prostitute
jokes.punchline: Comparing show business to prostitution
jokes.explanation: Classic Hollywood self-awareness - the comparison is apt and shows Hank's desperation for connection.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm in show business, so I'm a little bit of a whore, too.

jokes.text: You calling me a whore? I'm not calling you a whore. No, no. I'm just saying that, you know, you're different from the other wh... from the other girls.
jokes.setup: Hank's clumsy attempt at connection
jokes.punchline: He almost calls her a whore again while denying it
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Hank's complete inability to navigate social situations even in this context.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: You calling me a whore?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm not calling you a whore. No, no. I'm just saying that, you know, you're different from the other wh... from the other girls.

jokes.text: One time I did this guy named... uh, what's his name? Andy Richter. Get out.
jokes.setup: She mentions another sidekick client
jokes.punchline: It's Andy Richter, Conan's sidekick
jokes.explanation: Brilliant industry joke - naming a real sidekick creates a perfect comedy ecosystem reference.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: One time I did this guy named... uh, what's his name? Andy Richter.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Get out.

jokes.text: God, you sidekicks are all alike.
jokes.setup: Hank kicks her out like Andy Richter did
jokes.punchline: She categorizes sidekicks as a type of client
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to the sidekick theme - they're such a specific type that even prostitutes can categorize them.

dialogue.speaker: Prostitute
dialogue.line: God, you sidekicks are all alike.

jokes.text: A friend of mine said that he saw Mr. Kingsley sitting in a hotel bar with a prostitute. Your friend is mistaken.
jokes.setup: Network concerned about Hank's behavior
jokes.punchline: Artie's flat denial of obvious truth
jokes.explanation: Classic TV producer denial - Artie protecting his talent with transparent lies.

dialogue.speaker: Network Executive
dialogue.line: A friend of mine said that he saw Mr. Kingsley sitting in a hotel bar with a prostitute.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Your friend is mistaken.

jokes.text: He sees a bald man with a hooker. Automatically, it's Hank Kingsley. I wasn't aware Carl Reiner's out of town.
jokes.setup: Defending Hank against the accusation
jokes.punchline: Suggesting it could be another bald celebrity
jokes.explanation: Great deflection technique and a solid celebrity reference. Classic Artie problem-solving through misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He sees a bald man with a hooker. Automatically, it's Hank Kingsley. I wasn't aware Carl Reiner's out of town.

jokes.text: You only care about me because you're my agent. I pay you 10% to care about me. 7½%, you cheap bastard.
jokes.setup: Hank accuses his agent of only caring for money
jokes.punchline: The agent corrects the commission percentage while being insulted
jokes.explanation: Perfect agent behavior - even while being emotionally honest, he corrects the business details.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You only care about me because you're my agent. I pay you 10% to care about me.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: 7½%, you cheap bastard.

jokes.text: You've been married to the same lady for 56 years. I'm thinking of getting out.
jokes.setup: Hank questions Sid's understanding of divorce
jokes.punchline: After 56 years, he's 'thinking' of leaving
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery - the casual tone about ending a 56-year marriage is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: You've been married to the same lady for 56 years. I'm thinking of getting out.

jokes.text: I did him once. In 1956. It was only the one time. Now we're dear, dear friends.
jokes.setup: Helen talks about knowing Sid for 40 years
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation about their sexual history
jokes.explanation: Unexpected revelation delivered matter-of-factly. Shows the casual nature of Hollywood relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: I did him once. In 1956. It was only the one time. Now we're dear, dear friends.

jokes.text: Darlene, he's hit bottom and broke through to another bottom I know nothing about.
jokes.setup: Asked about Hank's condition
jokes.punchline: There are levels of rock bottom below rock bottom
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor for depression/addiction. The phrase 'broke through to another bottom' is comedy gold and deeply quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Darlene, he's hit bottom and broke through to another bottom I know nothing about.

jokes.text: Great show last night. You were really funny, as always. Hold on. What's your name? Raoul.
jokes.setup: Hotel worker compliments Hank on the show
jokes.punchline: Hank wasn't on the show but the guy didn't notice
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures the nightmare of being replaceable - he's been gone all week and nobody noticed.

dialogue.speaker: Raoul
dialogue.line: Great show last night. You were really funny, as always. Hold on. What's your name? Raoul.

jokes.text: And that is the effect, apparently, that I'm having on the American fucking viewing public!
jokes.setup: Raoul didn't notice he wasn't on the show
jokes.punchline: Hank's breakdown about his irrelevance
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Hank's existential crisis boiling over. The formal language makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And that is the effect, apparently, that I'm having on the American fucking viewing public!

jokes.text: Raul Julia was here? Raoul the waiter.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions 'Raoul'
jokes.punchline: Artie thinks it's the famous actor Raul Julia
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Artie's Hollywood mindset - every name sounds like a celebrity to him.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Raul Julia was here?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Raoul the waiter.

jokes.text: This is a Beretta 9mm Centurion. You just pick that baby up. It'll do the job for you.
jokes.setup: Hank wants to end it all
jokes.punchline: Artie's detailed knowledge and enthusiasm about the suicide gun
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - Artie's matter-of-fact approach to suicide. The specific gun model adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: This is a Beretta 9mm Centurion. You just pick that baby up. It'll do the job for you.

jokes.text: Listen, I'm gonna give housekeeping 20 bucks. I mean, you won't feel a fucking thing, but they're the ones who are gonna have to suffer.
jokes.setup: Encouraging Hank to use the gun
jokes.punchline: Artie's practical concern for the cleaning staff
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant dark comedy - Artie's consideration for housekeeping in a suicide scenario is perfectly twisted and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Listen, I'm gonna give housekeeping 20 bucks. I mean, you won't feel a fucking thing, but they're the ones who are gonna have to suffer.

jokes.text: No one cares because I'm the boss. The boss has no problems. Sometimes, I swear to God, I feel like I should just end it all.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about his problems
jokes.punchline: The irony of him having the same suicidal thoughts as Hank
jokes.explanation: Good character parallel - Larry's self-pity mirrors Hank's crisis, showing their similar neuroses.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No one cares because I'm the boss. The boss has no problems. Sometimes, I swear to God, I feel like I should just end it all.

jokes.text: You can always blow your head off. Safety's off. Ready to go. The divorce gun.
jokes.setup: Larry's suicidal ideation
jokes.punchline: Artie offers the same gun, calling it 'the divorce gun'
jokes.explanation: 'The divorce gun' as a casual tool for marital problems is darkly hilarious. Great callback that shows Artie's consistent worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You can always blow your head off. Safety's off. Ready to go. The divorce gun.

jokes.text: How close did Hank come to shooting himself? Oh, that fucking baby. He started crying the second I pulled it out.
jokes.setup: Asked about Hank's suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissive response calling Hank a baby
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - completely unsentimental about suicide attempts. His disappointment in Hank's lack of follow-through is brilliantly cold.

dialogue.line: How close did Hank come to shooting himself?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, that fucking baby. He started crying the second I pulled it out.

jokes.text: Gosh, this brings back memories.
jokes.setup: Larry handling the gun
jokes.punchline: Nostalgic tone about suicide weapon
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry casual darkness - treating suicide gun with warm nostalgia

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Gosh, this brings back memories.
